title: Moving back to a smaller city after twelve years away
summary: Reflect on the contested choice of moving back to a smaller home city after more than a decade in larger ones, and what that decision revealed about ambition, scale, and belonging.
topic: Returning to a smaller home city after years in larger ones
prompt: Write 3 paragraphs (350–500 words) about returning to a smaller home city after more than a decade in larger ones (or a friend's similar move), and reflect on what that decision revealed about ambition, scale, and belonging. Use the past perfect, second conditional, present perfect for reflection, and 'however' or 'although'.
hintStarters: I moved back to Riga at thirty-four, after twelve years in three larger cities, and almost everyone I told assumed I was either burnt out or had run out of options, neither of which was quite…
If you had asked me at twenty-two whether I could imagine living in my home city as an adult, I would have said no with complete confidence, because…
Although a smaller city offers obvious trade-offs in salary and variety, what I have come to think it actually returns to you is something most lists of pros and cons fail to count, which is…

hintVocab.phrase: to know everyone at the bakery
hintVocab.meaningVi: biết mọi người ở tiệm bánh
kết nối lại với gia đình

hintVocab.phrase: the version of yourself you become
hintVocab.meaningVi: phiên bản của bạn mà bạn trở thành

sampleText:
I moved back to Riga at thirty-four, after twelve years in three larger cities, and almost everyone I told assumed I was either burnt out or had run out of options. Neither of these was quite right. I had been doing well in the last of those cities; the salary had been the highest I had ever earned, and a promotion was being discussed when I gave notice. What had begun to bother me, slowly, was the version of myself I was becoming in order to keep doing well there. I had been working longer hours than I wanted, declining family events I had no real reason to miss, and noticing — with a small, persistent quietness — that I had not had a conversation with anyone in my building for more than two years. The move was not a retreat from ambition. It was a different definition of what I wanted to be good at.

If you had asked me at twenty-two whether I could imagine living in Riga as an adult, I would have said no with complete confidence. The city had felt too familiar, too connected to versions of me I wanted to outgrow. Yet I am now living in a flat ten minutes from where I went to secondary school, and the experience has been almost nothing like what I expected. I am not bored. I am not nostalgic. Although the city itself has not changed dramatically in twelve years, I have changed enough that the same streets read differently. The bakery on the corner is still owned by the same woman, who now greets me by name and asks about my parents. The river still freezes at the edges in February. What is new is that I notice these things in a way I could not have noticed them at twenty-two, because I had not yet known what their absence would feel like.

What I have come to believe, after almost two years of being back, is that the decision was not about size or salary or even ambition. It was about which version of myself I was willing to spend the next decade becoming. A larger city had been quietly shaping me into a person who treated relationships as scheduling problems and weekends as recovery time. A smaller city, with fewer opportunities to perform a successful life, leaves more space for the kind of life that does not need an audience. However, I do not want to pretend this is the right move for everyone. There are people for whom a smaller city would be genuinely too small, whose work or temperament needs the friction of a larger place. What I would say carefully is that 'bigger' is not automatically a measure of progress, and noticing this in time may be the most useful thing a decade in larger cities can give you.

sampleGrammarNotes.title: Third conditional for past hypothetical questions
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyVi: 'If you had asked me at twenty-two whether I could imagine living in Riga as an adult, I would have said no with complete confidence' dùng điều kiện loại 3 để mô tả một câu hỏi không được hỏi và một câu trả lời không được nói. Đây là cách thanh lịch để diễn đạt 'người tôi từng là'.
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyEn: 'If you had asked me at twenty-two whether I could imagine living in Riga as an adult, I would have said no with complete confidence' uses the third conditional to describe a question that was not asked and an answer that was not given. This is an elegant way to express 'the person I used to be'.

sampleGrammarNotes.title: Past perfect continuous for habits that had formed over years
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyVi: 'I had been working longer hours than I wanted' dùng quá khứ hoàn thành tiếp diễn để diễn đạt một thói quen đã hình thành trong một thời gian dài trước thời điểm chính. Cấu trúc này phù hợp với văn phản tỉnh vì nó nhấn mạnh tính lâu dài, tích lũy.
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyEn: 'I had been working longer hours than I wanted' uses the past perfect continuous for a habit that had formed over a long time before the main moment. This structure fits reflective writing because it emphasises duration and accumulation.

sampleTranslationVi:
Tôi chuyển về Riga ở tuổi ba mươi tư, sau mười hai năm ở ba thành phố lớn hơn, và hầu hết mọi người tôi kể đều cho rằng tôi hoặc đã kiệt sức hoặc đã hết lựa chọn. Không điều nào trong số đó hoàn toàn đúng. Tôi đã làm việc tốt ở thành phố cuối cùng trong số đó; lương đã là cao nhất tôi từng kiếm, và việc thăng chức đang được bàn đến khi tôi nộp đơn xin nghỉ. Điều đã bắt đầu làm tôi phiền lòng, chậm rãi, là phiên bản của chính mình mà tôi đang trở thành để tiếp tục làm tốt ở đó. Tôi đã làm việc nhiều giờ hơn tôi muốn, từ chối các sự kiện gia đình mà tôi không có lý do thực sự để bỏ lỡ, và nhận ra — với một sự lặng lẽ nhỏ và dai dẳng — rằng tôi đã không có cuộc trò chuyện với bất kỳ ai trong tòa nhà của tôi trong hơn hai năm. Việc chuyển nhà không phải là sự rút lui khỏi tham vọng. Đó là một định nghĩa khác về điều tôi muốn giỏi.

Nếu bạn đã hỏi tôi ở tuổi hai mươi hai liệu tôi có thể tưởng tượng sống ở Riga khi trưởng thành, tôi đã sẽ nói không với sự tự tin hoàn toàn. Thành phố đã cảm thấy quá quen thuộc, quá gắn liền với những phiên bản của tôi mà tôi muốn vượt qua. Vậy mà tôi giờ đang sống trong một căn hộ cách trường trung học tôi từng học mười phút, và trải nghiệm gần như không hề giống điều tôi mong đợi. Tôi không chán. Tôi không hoài niệm. Mặc dù chính thành phố không thay đổi mạnh mẽ trong mười hai năm, tôi đã thay đổi đủ để cùng những con phố đó được đọc theo cách khác. Tiệm bánh ở góc phố vẫn thuộc về cùng người phụ nữ đó, người giờ chào tôi bằng tên và hỏi thăm về bố mẹ tôi. Con sông vẫn đóng băng ở các mép vào tháng Hai. Điều mới là tôi nhận ra những điều này theo cách tôi đã không thể nhận ra ở tuổi hai mươi hai, bởi vì tôi chưa từng biết sự vắng mặt của chúng sẽ cảm thấy như thế nào.

Điều tôi đã dần tin, sau gần hai năm quay về, là quyết định không phải về kích thước hay lương hay thậm chí tham vọng. Đó là về phiên bản nào của chính mình mà tôi sẵn sàng dành thập kỷ tới để trở thành. Một thành phố lớn hơn đã lặng lẽ định hình tôi thành một người đối xử với các mối quan hệ như những vấn đề lên lịch và cuối tuần như thời gian phục hồi. Một thành phố nhỏ hơn, với ít cơ hội hơn để diễn một cuộc sống thành công, để lại nhiều không gian hơn cho loại cuộc sống không cần khán giả. Tuy nhiên, tôi không muốn giả vờ đây là động thái đúng cho tất cả mọi người. Có những người mà một thành phố nhỏ hơn sẽ thực sự quá nhỏ, công việc hay khí chất của họ cần sự cọ sát của một nơi lớn hơn. Điều tôi sẽ nói cẩn thận là 'lớn hơn' không tự động là thước đo của sự tiến bộ, và nhận ra điều này kịp thời có thể là điều hữu ích nhất một thập kỷ ở các thành phố lớn có thể cho bạn.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses the third conditional correctly?
options: If you had asked me at twenty-two, I would have said no with complete confidence.
If you asked me at twenty-two, I would have said no with complete confidence.
If you had asked me at twenty-two, I would say no with complete confidence.
If you had asked me at twenty-two, I would have say no with complete confidence.
mcQuestions.explanation: Third conditional: 'had + past participle' in the if-clause; 'would have + past participle' in the main clause.
mcQuestions.hint: 'If + had + past participle, would have + past participle'.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses the past perfect continuous correctly?
options: I had been working longer hours than I wanted.
I has been working longer hours than I wanted.
I had being working longer hours than I wanted.
I had worked longer hours than I wanted continuously.
Although the city itself has not changed dramatically in twelve years, I have changed enough that the same streets read differently.
Although the city itself has not changed dramatically in twelve years, but I have changed enough that the same streets read differently.
Although, the city itself has not changed dramatically in twelve years I have changed enough that the same streets read differently.
The city itself has not changed dramatically in twelve years, although however I have changed enough that the same streets read differently.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Although + clause, main clause' — no 'but', no comma after 'although', no doubling.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which option uses the past perfect correctly?
options: I had not had a conversation with anyone in my building for more than two years.
I have not had a conversation with anyone in my building for more than two years at that point.
I had not have a conversation with anyone in my building for more than two years.
I had not been have a conversation with anyone in my building for more than two years.
mcQuestions.explanation: Past perfect = 'had + past participle'; past participle of 'have' is 'had'.
mcQuestions.hint: Past perfect = 'had + past participle'; past participle of 'have' is 'had'.

options: Almost everyone I told assumed I was either burnt out or had run out of options.
Almost everyone I told assumed I was either burnt out nor had run out of options.
Almost everyone I told assumed I was either burnt out and had run out of options.
Almost everyone I told assumed I was both burnt out or had run out of options.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Either X or Y' for one of two alternatives; 'nor' goes with 'neither'; 'and' is wrong with 'either'.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which option uses 'be willing to + base verb' correctly?
options: Which version of myself I was willing to spend the next decade becoming.
Which version of myself I was willing spending the next decade becoming.
Which version of myself I was willing for spend the next decade becoming.
Which version of myself I was willing to spent the next decade becoming.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Be willing to + base verb' is the standard structure.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses the present perfect correctly?
options: What I have come to believe, after almost two years of being back, is that the decision was not about size.
What I came to believe, after almost two years of being back, is that the decision was not about size.
What I have came to believe, after almost two years of being back, is that the decision was not about size.
What I have come believing, after almost two years of being back, is that the decision was not about size.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which option correctly uses 'treat X as Y'?
options: A larger city had been quietly shaping me into a person who treated relationships as scheduling problems.
A larger city had been quietly shaping me into a person who treated relationships like as scheduling problems.
A larger city had been quietly shaping me into a person who treated relationships for scheduling problems.
A larger city had been quietly shaping me into a person who treated relationships to scheduling problems.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Treat X as Y' is the correct collocation; 'like as', 'for', 'to' are wrong.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses 'fewer opportunities + to + base verb' correctly?
options: A smaller city, with fewer opportunities to perform a successful life, leaves more space.
A smaller city, with less opportunities to perform a successful life, leaves more space.
A smaller city, with fewer opportunities for perform a successful life, leaves more space.
A smaller city, with fewer opportunities to performing a successful life, leaves more space.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which option correctly uses 'be being + past participle' (past continuous passive)?
options: A promotion was being discussed when I gave notice.
A promotion was being discuss when I gave notice.
A promotion was discussing when I gave notice.
A promotion has being discussed when I gave notice.
mcQuestions.explanation: Past continuous passive = 'was/were being + past participle'.
mcQuestions.hint: Past continuous passive = 'was/were + being + past participle'.

options: Noticing this in time may be the most useful thing a decade in larger cities can give you.
Noticing this on time may be the most useful thing a decade in larger cities can give you.
Noticing this at time may be the most useful thing a decade in larger cities can give you.
Noticing this by time may be the most useful thing a decade in larger cities can give you.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'In time' = soon enough; 'on time' = at the scheduled moment; 'at time' and 'by time' are not standard phrases.
mcQuestions.hint: 'In time' = soon enough; 'on time' = at the scheduled moment.

criticalThinkingQuestion: If 'bigger' is not automatically a measure of progress, what would you propose instead as a way to know whether a place is genuinely serving the person you are trying to become — and how would you know without first leaving it?
